data.title: The pope visits Hong Kong for 3 hours in 1970 – from the SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on December 5, 1970
By Staff reporters
A red letter day for Hong Kong Catholics
The 73-year-old pontiff, looking fit in spite of his strenuous Asian tour, responded warmly. At times he stretched out his arms in a symbol of loving embrace, at others he blessed the crowds gathered to see him.
He seemed especially pleased by the spontaneous gaiety of the greeting which 20,000 Catholic children gave him at the Happy Valley Racecourse to which he was taken by helicopter after his arrival at Kai Tak.
Message of love
In spite of the drizzle and a cold wind, he asked for the closed top of the car to be opened. He then stood up so that the children could see him.
At this, the cheers merged into a continuous, thunderous roar.
The expression of love – the main theme of the pope’s Asian tour – was also the message he conveyed to all Chinese people, including those in China, in his much-awaited farewell speech at Kai Tak Airport after the mass at the Government Stadium.
“Christ is a teacher, a shepherd and a loving redeemer for China too,” he declared.
“The Church cannot leave unsaid this good word: love, which will be forever.”
For Hong Kong, too, he had special words. In his airport broadcast he referred to the vast progress Hong Kong had made in recent years and added:
“We shall invoke God’s blessing on your continued efforts to promote justice, prosperity and peace.”

As his special aircraft took him away, His Holiness sent a message to North Vietnam expressing the wish “that the sincere efforts of the interested parties may promptly lead to the just peace so greatly desired”.
The pope was greeted at his arrival at the airport by the Roman Catholic Bishop of Hong Kong, Monsignor Francis Hsu, by the acting governor, Sir Hugh Norman-Walker, and by the colony’s leading citizens.
From there, the pope and his entourage were flown in four helicopters to the racecourse where shouts of “Viva il Papa” added to the thunderous ovation.
After his racecourse tour, the pope was driven to the Government Stadium when 50,000 people gave him another tremendous welcome.
The royal procession around the arena in an open jeep was greeted by elderly folk clapping hands entwined with rosaries, flag-waving children and teenagers uttering shrieks and screams usually accorded “pop” stars.
All were craning forward to see the pale countenance, the lifted hands and heart-warming smile. On the far side of the arena, youngsters became almost delirious with excitement.
The Holy Father became infected with this overwhelming enthusiasm and he blew kisses, made the sign of the cross and raised his arms wide as if he would enfold this multitude of Christians to his heart. This was perhaps the most moving moment.
The jeep edged past the guard of honour. Tired and cramps forgotten, the scouts and guides had their moment of glory.

And descending from the jeep, the pope went first to the purple-clad Anglican Bishop, the Right Reverend Gilbert Baker, who with Mrs Baker, received warm handshakes and cordial greetings.
Other guests of honour were met in turn: other clergy; then holders of papal honours; the disabled in wheelchairs; and members of religious orders standing four-square around the central altar.
Everywhere the pope went, Bishop Hsu was at his elbow. The 50-year-old bishop, whose appointment to head the Hong Kong diocese was made less than 18 months ago, introduced most of the dignitaries to His Holiness, called his attention and blessing to special groups, and guided him to all the right places at the right times.
The slender pontiff was escorted to the steps of the altar, where he donned green and ivory silk vestments for the celebration of the Papal High Mass.
Prayers and petitions, bilingual and reverberating around the stadium, were in progress when the rain commenced to fall steadily.
Something in the atmosphere reminded English folk present of the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, when drenched crowds stood patiently listening to the consecration prayers relayed from Westminster Abbey.
During the offertory presentation umbrellas went up in colourful array. But the people who had heard Paul say he was happy to be with his sons and daughters in Hong Kong – “the largest Chinese diocese in the world” – stood spellbound.
The pope’s visit to Hong Kong was “all too brief” in the words of the acting governor, Norman-Walker, who welcomed him just before 1.30pm and said goodbye barely three hours later.
But those few hours were enough to give Hong Kong’s 247,000 Catholics a great spiritual uplift, for although they made up the world’s largest Chinese Catholic community, they have never before been visited by the head of their church.
The visit did much good for the colony’s church-unity movement, which is one of the world’s strongest. Catholic sources said that even before they had time to send out invitations to the papal mass, many leaders of other churches had expressed a desire to attend.
And praying with the pope and about 50 ministers and leaders of several religions was the Bishop Baker.
Bishop Baker further shored up the foundations of the Hong Kong ecumenical movement yesterday with a letter to Paul. In it, he expressed admiration for the pontiff, who had undertaken such an “arduous apostolic and missionary journey”, and said Hong Kong Anglicans were praying that, through the pope’s labours, the “message of Christ” might be more deeply understood by the people of the Far East.
Strenuous
It was a strenuous three hours for the 73-year-old pontiff. But throughout, he looked well and walked with a spry step. Between his flowing white robes and white skullcap, his face looked sun-tanned and had a warm glow of health.
Paul usually speaks softly and slowly. But yesterday while preaching to just over 50,000 people in the Government Stadium, his amplified voice sounded very tired, almost weak, and he faltered over many of the English words that don’t come easily to him. There were however, three words that he pronounced with heavy emphasis, every syllable distinct.
The words came at the end of a sentence, at the end of his sermon: “Christ is a teacher, a shepherd and a loving redeemer FOR CHINA TOO.”
His Holiness habitually emphasises the last words of a sentence, but this time he was so forceful – far more so than in any other part of his sermon – that it was clear he wanted his message of love and peace to reach Chinese everywhere.
That however, was virtually all he said about China. He had decided to restrain his public expressions of love and concern for the Chinese people, although Catholic sources said he had privately compared his 1970 Far East mission to that of Francis Xavier, the missionary who died on the island of Shangchuan in 1552 while trying to get into China.

data.title: Chinese families ‘hope truth will come out’ when hunt for missing flight MH370 resumes
data.description:
Malaysia’s announcement that the search for missing Malaysia Airlines flight 370 will resume was welcome news for Jiang Hui, whose mother was a passenger on the plane.
“I hope the truth will come out soon, the wait has been such a torment for the families,” he wrote in a social media post sharing the news.
Malaysia’s transport ministry made the announcement on Wednesday, more than a decade after the Boeing 777 carrying 227 passengers and 12 crew vanished en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing in one of aviation’s biggest mysteries.
Malaysian authorities said the search – which began in March in the southern Indian Ocean but was suspended after a few weeks due to poor weather – would resume on December 30.
It is being led by US-based exploration firm Ocean Infinity under a “no find, no fee” arrangement.


“The families welcome the continued search from the Malaysian government and Ocean Infinity,” said Jiang, whose mother Jiang Cuiyun was one of more than 150 Chinese passengers on the flight.
“We hope the search on reward can be changed into an open and long-term one, with no fixed end date. The families would be willing to participate in such a reward search.”
He also said he hoped more Chinese could join the search.
The announcement has renewed hopes among relatives like Jiang that they might finally get some closure.
Li Eryou, a farmer in Hubei province whose son was on the flight, wrote on social media that “the authorities need to tell the families – after 11 troubling years – what exactly happened”.
“When the 2018 safety report said investigators could not be sure of the real reason that MH370 went missing it was an insult for all the families and those who were following this,” he added.
Two-thirds of the passengers on the flight were Chinese. There were also Malaysian, French, Australian, Indonesian, Indian, American, Ukrainian and Canadian nationals, among others.
After the plane disappeared without trace in 2014, the Chinese families formed advocacy groups to push for the search to continue and negotiate with the Chinese and Malaysian governments.
Over the past 11 years, relatives have shown up at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Beijing every week to ask for updates and to call for more to be done to find the wreckage of the plane.
They also regularly visit temples in the Chinese capital to pray for their lost family members.


Jiang, who is the organiser of a WeChat group for relatives, has taken part in efforts to find the plane, including a 2016 trip to Madagascar organised by support group Voice 370 to search for plane debris.
He said he was trying to push the search forward in three ways – through public opinion, legal action and diplomatic pressure.
A Beijing court began hearing compensation claims from the families in 2023. Jiang and about 40 other families are suing Malaysia Airlines, its insurer, Boeing and the manufacturer of the plane’s engine.
They are seeking compensation, an apology, psychological assistance and funding to continue the search for the plane.
“To be optimistic, we might see results by the end of this year,” Jiang said.
Others are using social media to share news on aviation safety or any new findings that could be relevant to the plane’s whereabouts.
Li, the farmer, has written some 3,000 poems for his missing son, which he has also posted on social media.
“Let’s meet again, my child, when the frost of spring still hangs, when the wind has not yet blown away the leaves, when my aged arms and legs can still move,” he wrote in February.

data.title: Drink in Focus: Bancha & Chestnut Alexander at Madara
data.description:
At the end of a long year, a comforting cocktail and human connection are always in order, especially during the colder winter months. While most might seek a hot toddy, an Irish coffee or even festive eggnog, the velvety, rich profile of the classic brandy Alexander – combining cognac, crème de cacao and cream – is an underrated option for something sweet and satisfying.
Few places riff on the brandy Alexander quite like Madara, an extension of modern izakaya Yorucho in Causeway Bay. Co-founder Rayven Leung and his team build on the success of Leung’s other venue, Takumi Mixology Salon, by focusing on bespoke cocktails alongside signatures focused on tea, fruits and – more unusually – Shakespeare. The first ingredient was the starting point for the Bancha & Chestnut Alexander.
Leung, who usually prefers to limit the number of ingredients in a cocktail, pushes himself to his maximum of six for this drink: roasted bancha green tea leaves, cognac, a cask-aged mugi (barley) shochu, adzuki liqueur, Sakari matcha liqueur, and cream.

The observant may spot the glaring omission of chestnut as an ingredient. “Hongkongers,” Leung explains, “rarely order cocktails [like the brandy Alexander] that may be too sweet or creamy because they might be heavy. We changed the name so guests can focus on the flavour profile – it allows us to share how we can use bancha and mugi shochu to evoke the flavour of charcoal-roasted chestnuts without actually including them in the cocktail.”
Everything about the cocktail – from ingredients and assembly to presentation and serving vessel – is tailored to evoke charcoal-roasted chestnuts, that local winter treat sold from street carts.
“Bancha has the exact same natural fragrance [as] fresh chestnuts,” Leung notes, adding that the leaves also have “that charcoal character, which is different from, say, chestnut paste on cakes or chestnut extracts or syrups”.
Bancha green tea leaves are harvested at a more mature stage than sencha, and thus have a heartier, earthier flavour. The leaves are roasted in front of the guest to release their aroma during assembly as part of the presentation.

“The fragrance is key because it helps the guest mentally hone in on the flavour in focus,” Leung explains. “There’s a fresh chestnut fragrance and flavour that bancha and cask-aged mugi shochu pair well to reproduce. In Japan, this is a common approach, but it’s not often seen in Hong Kong. The cask maturation of the mugi shochu adds depth and increases the complexity of that fresh chestnut flavour.”
Cognac is added partly due to its use in the classic brandy Alexander, but also to supplement the alcohol content of the shochu and round out the flavour of the bancha. Matcha and adzuki liqueurs, along with the cream, help refine and lengthen the sweet finish of the drink.
“There are a lot of details that affect the final result and experience of a cocktail,” Leung notes. “It comes down to whether a mixologist can control these things, and if they understand why they use certain shaking methods, serving temperatures and so on.”

Leung has partnered with local ceramic gallery and studio Waka Artisans to use their ceramic tea cups as serving vessels. “Our main concern is how the guest drinks from a cup and how ingredients linger, or don’t linger, when sipping,” he says. “These ceramic cups are quite wide, which allows a drink to sweep over the whole palate – great for drinks with complex flavour profiles. What the guest might end up doing is licking their lips when finishing a sip – something that suits tea.”
The Bancha & Chestnut Alexander is actually the result of a guest’s bespoke request – an aspect key to the sense of comfort the drink brings.
“Whenever we make a cocktail, I aim to understand what the guest really needs – whether they are cold or warm, happy or sad; if they need company or solace,” says Leung. “What makes me happiest is when guests give us a unique challenge or prompt. This [pushes] us to think creatively and try things we’ve never tried before. If you share the challenge of creativity with the guest, you’ll open up new possibilities. We treat that type of trust very seriously.”
Anatomy of a cocktail
Name: Bancha & Chestnut Alexander
Bar: Madara, 16/F, Circle Tower, 28 Tang Lung Street, Causeway Bay
Price: HK$185
Main ingredients: The Hachi sherry-cask-matured mugi shochu, bancha tea leaves, cognac, Sakari matcha liqueur, adzuki liqueur, cream
Presentation: Served in a ceramic Waka Artisans cup, garnished with orange and lime peel
Flavours: Earthy, sweet, fragrant, herbaceous, nutty, creamy

data.title: Xi Jinping urges Emmanuel Macron to ‘firmly stand on the right side of history’
data.description:
Chinese President Xi Jinping told his French counterpart Emmanuel Macron that he was willing to work with France to “eliminate interference” and work on developing a steady relationship as they met for talks on Thursday, where issues such as trade are expected to be on the agenda.
Xi said the world was undergoing rapid changes and the two countries “should demonstrate their responsibility, uphold the banner of multilateralism… and firmly stand on the right side of history”.
“China, together with France, has always acted in the fundamental interests of the two peoples and long-term interests of the international community, eliminating various interferences, adhering to equal dialogue and open cooperation,” he said.
Xi added that doing so could “more steadily” strengthen ties and contribute to the promotion of an equal and orderly multipolar world.
“China and France are both farsighted and responsible independent major powers, and constructive forces promoting a multipolar world,” he said.
Macron said that the two countries would continue to move forward amid geopolitical uncertainties. He admitted that there would be disagreements “but we have a responsibility to know how to transcend them and find cooperative mechanisms to resolve differences”.
The French leader is on a three-day trip to China – his fourth visit to the country – that will take him from Beijing to Chengdu in the southwestern province of Sichuan. He is expected to meet the Chinese president again on Friday.
Earlier on Thursday, Xi welcomed Macron to the Great Hall of the People with a ceremony that saw the French leader inspecting a guard of honour as children waved the flags of the two countries. The two leaders shook hands and exchanged brief remarks before heading into closed-door talks.
More to follow...

data.title: 89-year-old pastry chef’s ingredient of success: working with his son and grandson
data.description:
With more than 60 years of baking under his belt, 89-year-old pastry chef Yasuo Ono has a tried-and-true recipe for success: keep it simple and change with the times.
Ono starts baking at his cake shop, Emily, every morning at 7.30am, making items like apple pie and Mont Blanc desserts. He will turn 90 this month, but he is not thinking of retiring.
In early July, about 25 varieties of cakes, including seasonal offerings featuring peaches and mangoes as well as classic shortcakes, were displayed in the showcase alongside handwritten signs.
But Ono is not alone – he has his family helping him.

About half of small businesses in Japan have no successor to run them after the current generation steps down, according to the government, a problem that threatens not only local communities but the preservation of traditional crafts and skills.
Not so with Emily. Located in a quiet residential area in Owariasahi, in central Japan’s Aichi prefecture, Emily is now mainly operated by three generations: founder Ono, his 56-year-old son Yoshihide and his grandson Kazuki, 25.
“I was able to keep going because I love this,” Ono says.
Next year, Emily will mark its 60th anniversary. Ono still takes buses and subways on his days off to visit department stores in Nagoya and look at their cake offerings. “I look forward to seeing all the new things,” he says.
Ono was born in Niigata prefecture, northwest of Tokyo, in 1935. After graduating from junior high school, he moved to Tokyo, where he began living and working at a Western-style confectionery shop.
The young Ono had never eaten cake before, so when he tasted its buttercream richness for the first time, he thought, “So this is what cake tastes like.” He learned how to make cakes from scratch.

Ono opened his own cake shop in 1966 in Machida, in western Tokyo. After looking through books on sweets with his wife Mitsuko, now 85, and brainstorming together, they decided on the name Emily.
“It was easy to remember and sounded like a cake shop,” says Ono.
Emily is also the name of a chocolate cake that the shop has been making continuously since its founding.
In 1972, Ono moved to his current address in Owariasahi, with a population of 83,000, drawn by the presence of relatives and proximity to a municipal housing complex. Unable to initially sustain the business on cake sales, the shop primarily sold cigarettes and juice.
Around 2000, Yoshihide, Ono’s second son, who had finished training as a cake chef in Tokyo, took over as manager. This was despite Yoshihide having worked on motorcycle maintenance and having plans to open his own motorcycle business.
Yoshihide had felt it would be a shame to let his father’s cake shop go out of business, so he began learning pastry making at age 24. However, most customers came to buy cigarettes, and cake sales did not grow at first.
To offer a signature item, Yoshihide baked fresh cream puffs multiple times a day, developing them into a flagship item. At the time, frozen choux pastries were readily available, and their affordable price made them popular.

Then, around 2005, Emily began hosting cake-making classes – mainly for schoolchildren – which were well-received in the local community. Held about 100 times a year, they taught kids how to make cream puffs, cakes and other pastries.
The shop also began focusing on decorating cakes for display, introducing one creation after the next, such as cakes designed to resemble princess dresses.
The shop’s connection to local residents is its strength. For customers who order a birthday cake, the shop takes their photo when they come to pick it up, prints it on a postcard, and sends it with a handwritten congratulatory message.
The rising cost of wheat and other raw ingredients due to inflation is a challenge, but Yoshihide says, “We want to keep prices affordable so everyone can enjoy our cakes.”
The shop strives to keep prices low by minimising waste. Cakes are priced around 400 yen (US$2.50) each and the shop offers about 50 varieties of baked goods at affordable prices.

Yoshihide’s wife Yuri, 54, is particular about actively incorporating seasonal elements, such as using peaches harvested from her family home in Yamanashi, west of Tokyo.
Now, five pastry chefs, including Ono, bake pastries at the shop. Two years ago, Kazuki, the grandson, returned after completing his training in Tokyo.
“If we’re going to keep this going for 100 years, some things will need to keep changing with the times, but I want it to be a place where everyone feels welcome,” Kazuki says.
After closing time, the family discusses new products. Kazuki’s raisin butter sandwich, launched in May, has been a hit.
“Hearing ‘that’s delicious’ is the best reward,” Ono says, smiling. “I want people to enjoy something sweet when they feel a little tired.”

data.title: Huawei’s 2022 patent details novel technique to make 2-nm-class chips without EUV tool
data.description:
Huawei Technologies’ three-year-old patent for advanced patterning comparable with 2-nanometre grade technology without extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography tools has intensified speculation about a potential breakthrough in advanced chips.
The US-sanctioned company is working to patent a metal integration technique for manufacturing semiconductors, which allows narrow metal structures to be integrated using deep ultraviolet (DUV) technology even for “metal pitches below 21nm”, a feature required for 2nm-class chips.
The proposed solution offered a technical pathway to support a 2-nm process using older DUV technology, aiming to bypass US sanctions that block China’s access to the most advanced EUV tools from Dutch firm ASML.
The patent, currently pending, was originally submitted by Huawei in June 2022 and made public by China’s national intellectual property regulator in January this year. There is no evidence that the patent has been put to use.
Amid growing talk of China’s tech breakthroughs, a Chinese chip industry veteran argued that 14-nm logic chips could rival the performance of Nvidia’s 4-nm chips through integration with advanced memory and novel architecture.
Huawei declined to comment on the patent.

However, the patent has grabbed industry attention as it opens the possibility of overcoming a key obstacle to match Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC)’s 2-nm process. TSMC’s process is considered the most advanced in the world, although the technology has yet to be used for mass production.
Advanced lithography technology is essential for high-end chipmaking. It has proved to be a bottleneck for China’s ambitions, as the country is restricted from acquiring ASML’s top DUV and EUV systems due to US curbs.
Huawei’s solution, using a variation of self-aligned quadruple patterning (SAQP), employed an advanced “spacer-defined patterning” scheme with dual hard-mask materials to allow the creation of two sets of interwoven metal lines, reducing reliance on ultra-tight lithographic overlay.
It remains to be seen whether the complicated method, which requires more steps than EUV lithography, can work and achieve commercially viable yields for large-scale production.
The patent is part of Huawei’s ongoing efforts to seek breakthroughs in semiconductors, as the US-blacklisted company has been cut off from accessing advanced technologies, including high-end chips and tools needed to produce them.
In recent years, the Shenzhen-based company has submitted patents related to post-2-nm technologies, including 20 patents in 2023 on gate-all-around, a cutting-edge transistor for the 2-nm process, according to a report by Nikkei Xtech, which cited data from the Japanese patent analysis platform Patentfield.
There were also records for a complementary field-effect transistor, which is used for process nodes beyond 1nm, the report said.
Huawei has also seen a spike in patent applications for graphics processing units (GPUs) – the backbone for artificial intelligence development.
In 2023, Huawei filed 3,091 GPU-related patents – a tenfold increase from 2018. This total was also three times that of Intel and five times that of Nvidia.
A patent application filed by Huawei in September 2021, but made public last year, outlined the use of SAQP to “increase the design freedom of circuit patterns”. The process is similar to a patent granted to SiCarrier, a state-backed chip tool developer affiliated with Huawei.
SiCarier’s patent describes making chips on a 5-nm process node using DUV tools, the Post reported last year.

data.title: Scaffolding nets removed across Hong Kong as firms seek to meet 3-day deadline
data.description:
Construction firms have started removing scaffold netting across Hong Kong to meet a three-day government deadline, after suspected false safety certificates for mesh were found at two building sites in the wake of the deadly Tai Po fire that has claimed at least 159 lives.
The green mesh, commonly draped over bamboo scaffolding erected at buildings under construction or renovation, came under heavy scrutiny after authorities found that contractors had installed cheaper, non-fire-resistant netting alongside approved materials to deceive inspectors at Wang Fuk Court, accelerating the spread of the fire last week.
The government on Wednesday ordered the removal of all 200 scaffold nets across the city after a Shandong-based scaffolding netting manufacturer was alleged by authorities to have falsified safety certificates for materials used in housing renovation projects in Chai Wan and Fortress Hill.
On Thursday morning, workers were seen removing scaffolding mesh at a Kim Shin Lane development in Cheung Sha Wan, as well as at other places.
However, Tai On Building in Sai Wan Ho remained covered by scaffolding mesh in the morning. Residents there said they were concerned about potential fire risks following the Tai Po blaze, adding that the removal of the nets as required by the government would help eliminate the hazards.
Jenny Cheung, in her sixties, who has lived in the building for about 30 years, said the renovation work cost each household more than HK$50,000 (US$6,425) in total, and she had already paid about HK$20,000.
She said removing the mesh would make her family feel safer despite the delays it was expected to cause in the renovation.
“I have had this concern about scaffolding since the Tai Po fire,” she said. “Personal safety is the top priority after all.”

Speaking on a radio show earlier in the day, industry representatives said the decision to remove scaffolding nets was necessary to reassure residents that their safety was being considered, while the move was unlikely to hinder the progress of most projects.
Lawrence Ng San-wa, permanent honorary president of the Hong Kong Construction Sub‑Contractors Association, said the need to offer assurance to residents outweighed the cost of removing the mesh.
He added that the government’s new guidelines to be released next week, which will require on-site sampling and testing of netting by certified laboratories, would also alleviate residents’ concerns.
However, Ng said that the new procedures could create challenges in terms of storage and speed of construction work. He added that authorities could further improve the process by requiring mesh manufacturers to provide test reports upon delivery of their products, to reduce the burden on the local industry.
“Nowadays, for many cases in mainland China, quality assurance tests are only done once on products, and safety certificates are not necessarily relevant for every batch of them,” he said.
“We may consider asking the manufacturers to cooperate by doing tests on every delivery, so that we can leave most of the tasks to the factory side. When the products arrive in Hong Kong, we can be more assured, and the number of tests to be done locally and the time taken for them will be reduced.”

data.title: South Korea’s Kim Keon-hee feels ‘wronged in many ways’ as prosecutors seek 15-year jail term
data.description:
South Korea’s special prosecutors have sought a 15-year jail term for former first lady Kim Keon-hee, describing her as someone who has long been “beyond the reach of law enforcement”.
Kim, the wife of impeached ex-president Yoon Suk-yeol, faces multiple corruption allegations, including stock manipulation, bribery and illegal electioneering.
The court’s verdict is set for January 28.
The demand on Wednesday came about three months after Kim was indicted – just a year following Yoon’s botched December 3 martial law bid, which ultimately led to his impeachment and removal from office.
Kim, 53, was at the centre of a string of influence-peddling and bribery scandals throughout her husband’s term.
With a December 26 deadline looming for the special inquiry, prosecutors have accelerated investigations into additional allegations involving Kim.
“Only the defendant has existed outside the law, standing above it,” a special prosecutor said at the Seoul Central District Court.
Prosecutors sought a total of 15 years in prison, along with a 2 billion won (US$1.35 million) fine and 948 million won in forfeiture for three core charges.


Kim is accused of being involved in the manipulation of Deutsch Motors’ stock between 2010 and 2012, allegedly pocketing 811 million won in illicit profits.
She is also accused of receiving luxury goods worth roughly 80 million won in 2022 from a Unification Church official as bribes.
For these two charges, the special counsel requested 11 years in prison, a 2 billion won fine and forfeiture of 810 million won.
Additionally, she is suspected of receiving the results of free opinion surveys worth 270 million won with Yoon before the 2022 presidential election from a self-described political fixer.
In return, the couple allegedly helped secure a nomination for a parliamentary by-election that year. For this charge, prosecutors demanded four years in prison and forfeiture of 137 million won.
“All of the accomplices in the Deutsch Motors stock manipulation case have stood trial, but only the defendant remained an exception,” deputy special prosecutor Kim Hyeong-geun said during closing arguments.
“The entire nation watched with dismay. The defendant has undermined the South Korean judicial system.”
On the Unification Church bribery allegations, prosecutors said Kim Keon-hee violated “the constitutional principle of separation of religion and state” by colluding with the church to distort electoral fairness and damage democratic integrity.

Prosecutors argued that Kim showed no remorse, invoking her right to remain silent “not as a legal safeguard, but as a shield against revealing the truth”.
They justified their sentencing request by saying: “These acts will be remembered as a disgraceful destruction of the rule of law in our nation’s history. They far exceed what the existing sentencing guidelines – designed for typical offenders – can cover. Even the maximum sentences within the current guidelines are insufficient.”
Kim responded to the sentencing demand with a dry, incredulous snort.
“I feel very wronged in many ways, but I think I must have done many things wrong,” she said, before adding: “But what the special counsel said is a bit excessive … I think there is room for dispute.”
She then bowed and said: “I sincerely apologise for causing great concern to the public. I truly repent.”
Her defence team portrayed her as the target of a “politically motivated” investigation.
“If the special prosecutors claim Ms Kim destroyed the judicial system through the Deutsch Motors case, are they saying previous prosecutors [who cleared her] covered it up?” a defence lawyer told reporters.
During Yoon’s presidency, prosecutors questioned Kim over several corruption allegations but declined to indict her, citing insufficient evidence.


Mounting public anger over the perceived cover-up led the National Assembly – controlled by President Lee Jae-myung’s Democratic Party of Korea – to establish a separate special prosecutor team.
Lee won a snap election in June after Yoon was impeached and removed from office in April over his failed self-coup.
Beyond the three main charges for which sentencing has been sought, Kim faces additional bribery allegations.
She is accused of accepting Van Cleef & Arpels jewellery in 2022 from a businessman seeking a government post for his son-in-law.
Kim is also suspected of taking a Vacheron Constantin watch worth 50 million won from a major Yoon donor and a gold turtle figurine worth 6 million won from another supporter seeking a senior government appointment.
Prosecutors aim to add these charges before their mandate expires on December 26.
In a related development, President Lee on Tuesday urged authorities to examine if South Korean law should allow the dissolution of religious organisations implicated in political interference.
“We should examine institutional measures to enable dissolution orders for religious foundations, as seen in Japan,” he said.

Lee’s remarks follow allegations that the Unification Church bribed Kim and mobilised its members en masse to influence the 2023 party leadership election of Yoon’s conservative People Power Party. Calling the constitutional separation of church and state “truly critical”, he warned that its breach “undermines constitutional order”.
Japan’s courts ordered the dissolution of the Unification Church in March after the 2022 assassination of former prime minister Shinzo Abe brought the group under intense scrutiny.
The accused killer said he blamed Abe for fostering the church’s influence, which he claimed had financially devastated his family through excessive donations.
Referring to the Japanese precedent, Lee said: “We must also begin serious institutional reviews.”
If violations of church-state separation are left unchecked, he warned, “constitutional order will be shaken, and societal conflicts could escalate into something resembling religious wars”.

data.title: Kenya inquiry denounces ‘disturbing sexual misconduct’ at UK army base
data.description:
A Kenyan parliamentary inquiry has denounced a “disturbing trend of sexual misconduct” at a British Army base in the east African country “marked by rape, assault, and abandonment of children fathered by soldiers”.
Since Kenya’s independence in 1963, the former colonial power has maintained the British Army Training Unit Kenya (BATUK) on the outskirts of Nanyuki, about 200km (130 miles) north of the capital Nairobi.
The inquiry by the Kenyan parliament’s Defence Committee said there was little accountability for decades of sexual assaults by personnel from the base.
“Survivors of sexual violence reported cases being dropped or mishandled by local authorities, with many victims denied access to justice,” the lawmakers said, stating that their findings were based on numerous testimonies and more than a year of investigations.

The document, dated November 25, was made public on Tuesday, a few weeks after the arrest and detention in Britain of Robert James Purkiss, a 38-year-old soldier accused of the 2012 murder of Kenyan Agnes Wanjiru, a case that had long been a source of diplomatic tension between London and Nairobi.
Wanjiru was a 21-year-old mother when she was found dead in a septic tank at a hotel in Nanyuki.
Purkiss’ lawyer has said the former soldier “vehemently denies” the murder.
The Kenyan inquiry also found “consistent patterns of gross negligence by BATUK in the handling of unexploded ordnances, leading to multiple civilian injuries and fatalities”.
And it denounced significant environmental damage including “illegal dumping of military waste and toxic materials”.
“As a result, BATUK is increasingly seen as an occupying presence rather than a development partner, with affected residents drawing parallels to colonial injustices,” the report concluded.
The UK Ministry of Defence responded to the report.
“We deeply regret the challenges which have arisen in relation to our defence presence in Kenya,” the UK Ministry of Defence said in an emailed response to a request for comment. “Where new allegations have come to light in the Committee’s report, we stand ready to investigate those under our jurisdiction fully once evidence is provided.”
In 2003, Amnesty International claimed to have documented 650 rape allegations against British soldiers in Kenya between 1965 and 2001, denouncing “decades of impunity”.
Additional reporting by Bloomberg

data.title: Commercialisation of Hong Kong’s public venues must come with a caveat
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
The Leisure and Cultural Services Department’s plan to introduce market-driven operations at eight public venues, including the Hong Kong Cultural Centre piazza and Kai Tak Station Square, is a welcome move. Many government-run venues remain underused during weekdays, and professional operators could bring in new ideas, attract events and make better use of these taxpayer-funded public assets.
However, commercialisation must not come at the expense of the public interest. International experience shows that when public spaces are opened to private operators, safeguards such as a “community quota” are essential. Hong Kong should require operators to reserve affordable or free time slots for local arts groups, non-profit organisations and community events.
This is not simply a gesture of corporate social responsibility; it addresses a long-standing challenge many cultural groups face in securing suitable and reasonably priced venues.
These spaces should also be seen as strategic assets for Hong Kong’s ambition to build a more vibrant event economy. Better use of harbourfront venues, for example, could enhance visitor experiences. A convention held at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition and Centre in Wan Chai, followed by a harbourfront reception at the Hong Kong Museum of Art, connected by a boat ride on the Aqua Luna, would offer a distinctive experience that strengthens Hong Kong’s competitiveness as an events destination.
That said, the details will determine success. Not all venues should fall under a single operator, as landmark locations and community spaces serve different purposes and should be matched with operators who understand their unique audiences.
The proposed contract model – of an initial term of two or three years, with the possibility of a two-year extension – may also be too short to encourage meaningful investment. A longer-term commitment would give operators the certainty to enhance programming and facilities.
Transparency in rental terms and approval criteria is equally important, so both local and overseas organisers understand what types of activities are eligible.
Looking ahead, Hong Kong should seriously consider public-private partnerships (PPPs) as a sustainable framework for managing major public venues. The city’s past PPPs, from cross‑harbour tunnels to Hong Kong Disneyland, show that public oversight and private-sector expertise can work hand in hand.
A well-structured PPP would not only allow cost-sharing but also encourage deeper planning on a venue’s positioning, operational model and long-term development. Crucially, it would give the government a stronger mechanism to balance commercial vitality with community access, ensuring that public spaces continue to serve both economic goals and the broader public interest.
Roy Ying, senior lecturer of marketing, The Hang Seng University of Hong Kong
Our brave firefighters deserve their own day
A brave firefighter, Ho Wai-ho, passed away in the line of duty in the No 5 alarm fire at Wang Fuk Court on November 26. That Hong Kong can remain one of the safest cities in the world is in part attributed to the effort and contribution of the Fire Services Department. This fact is brought home by the tragedy.
Firefighting is dangerous work and occasionally may even cost lives. Hong Kong’s firefighters protect life and property so residents and visitors alike may go about their daily activities with confidence. Our firefighters’ gallantry, team spirit, devotion to duty and professionalism are highly creditable and recognised by the public.
We have a Teachers’ Day on September 10 and a Nurses’ Day on May 12 to recognise the important roles these professionals play. Why not a day for our firefighters? The government should also establish a Firefighters’ Day – say, on November 27, the day the Wang Fuk Court blaze was put out – to pay tribute to our firefighters. It will certainly boost the morale of the officers.
I would like to take this opportunity to pay tribute to the brave firefighter who died; his devotion to duty was exemplary. And I send my condolences to the family of the deceased.
The efforts of all those firefighters who helped put out the blaze are highly appreciated.
Barry Kwok Tak-ming, Wong Tai Sin
Click-bait news has no place in Hong Kong
There was hardly a person in Hong Kong who was not shocked by the scenes in Tai Po last week. And understandably the global media went into overdrive reporting on both the actual fires but also the human anguish from this awful event.
Some reports, however, decided to go political. One headline said there was “anger and tension at the scene of Hong Kong fire”, while another said “Hong Kong authorities order volunteers to pack up amidst fears of disruption”.
I’m all for good journalism but this form of click-bait news has no place in Hong Kong. It brought back memories of the Bloomberg article published in the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic, declaring Hong Kong to be a “failed state” as the writer observed a run on toilet paper.
Bob Rogers, Sai Kung
